coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots prepared by pyrolysis: Investigation of the luminescence mechanism and application as fluorescent probes
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Cunjin
Yang, Min
Shi, Huanxian
Yao, Zhuoru
Liu, Enzhou
Hu, Xiaoyun
Guo, Pengqi
Xue, Weiming
Fan, Jun
coredata.dc:description: In this work, the effect of reaction conditions on the preparation of CQDs by pyrolysis was studied. More specifically, CQDs-1 with single emission peak of blue fluorescence and CQDs-2 with double emission peak of yellow fluorescence were prepared by pyrolysis methods in a closed and open environment, respectively. The mechanism of the difference in optical properties of the two types of CQDs had been systematically studied. The results showed that the difference in the degree of graphitization and surface state was the main reason for the difference in the optical properties of the two types of CQDs. The CQDs-2 with dual emission characteristics was used for high sensitivity and high selectivity detection of cephalosporin antibiotics based on the inner-filter effect (IFE). The limit of detection (LOD) was 0.87–2.78 μM, with a good recovery rate (99.4–102%) in the milk spike recovery test. Additionally, the cytotoxicity and cell imaging showed that CQDs-1 and CQDs-2 have potential applications in biological systems.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Pyrolysis
Carbon quantum dots
Luminescence mechanism
Fluorescent probe
Cephalosporin antibiotics

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Cell imaging of CQDs-1 and CQDs-2
The influence of pyrolysis environment on the prepared CQDs
The dual emission fluorescent probe detection cephalosporin antibiotics
Mechanism of CQDs-2 detection of cephalosporins antibiotics
Detection of CRO in real sample

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Three dimensional fluorescence spectra of CQDs-1 (a) and CQDs-2 (b). (c) UV–Vis absorption spectrum of CQDs-1 and CQDs-2. (d) Excitation and emission spectra of CQDs-1 and CQDs-2.
The TEM images of CQDs-1 (a) and CQDs-2 (b), inset is the size distribution histogram. HRTEM images of CQDs-1 (c) and CQDs-2 (d).
XRD (a), Raman (b) and FT-IR (c) spectra of CQDs-1 and CQDs-2. (d) Fluorescence lifetime decay curve of CQDs-1 and CQDs-2.
XPS spectrum of CQDs-1 and CQDs-2. (a) XPS survey. (b) C 1s. (c) N 1s. (d) O 1s.

PL spectra at EX = 270 nm (left) and Linear graph (right) of the CQDs-2 solution upon addition of different concentrations of cephalosporin antibiotics. (a) CRO; (b) CTX; (c) CEX; (d) CF; (e) CFP and (f) CFM.

Three dimensional fluorescence spectra of CQDs-2 (a) and CQDs-2+CRO (b). (c) UV absorption spectra of CQDs-2 solution and cephalosporin antibiotic solution. (d) Fluorescence lifetime decay curve of CQDs-2 and CQDs-2+CRO.
Schematic diagram of the preparation of CQDs by pyrolysis methods in two different environments.
Comparison of proposed fluorescent probe with other methods for the detection of cephalosporin antibiotics.

row.entry: MIP-GQDs and MIP-CdTe QDs fluorescent probe
MIP-GQDs and MIP-CdTe QDs fluorescent probe

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Recoveries of CRO in spiked milk samples.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots prepared by pyrolysis: Investigation of the luminescence mechanism and application as fluorescent probes

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, Northwest University, Xi'an, 710069, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Engineering, Northwest University, Xi’an, 710069, P.R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Physics, Northwest University, Xi'an, 710127, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Physics, Northwest University, Xi’an 710127, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food Science and Engineering, Northwest University, Xi'an, 710069, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Food Science and Engineering, Northwest University, Xi’an 710069, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Shaanxi University of Chinese Medicine, Xianyang, 712046, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmacy, Shaanxi University of Chinese Medicine, Xianyang, 712046, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, the effect of reaction conditions on the preparation of CQDs by pyrolysis was studied. More specifically, CQDs-1 with single emission peak of blue fluorescence and CQDs-2 with double emission peak of yellow fluorescence were prepared by pyrolysis methods in a closed and open environment, respectively. The mechanism of the difference in optical properties of the two types of CQDs had been systematically studied. The results showed that the difference in the degree of graphitization and surface state was the main reason for the difference in the optical properties of the two types of CQDs. The CQDs-2 with dual emission characteristics was used for high sensitivity and high selectivity detection of cephalosporin antibiotics based on the inner-filter effect (IFE). The limit of detection (LOD) was 0.87–2.78 μM, with a good recovery rate (99.4–102%) in the milk spike recovery test. Additionally, the cytotoxicity and cell imaging showed that CQDs-1 and CQDs-2 have potential applications in biological systems.

ce:section.ce:para: Since CQDs were first discovered by Xu et al., in 2004 [1], they have been widely used in many fields such as fluorescent sensing, cell imaging, and optoelectronic devices due to their excellent optical properties, simple preparation process and low biological toxicity [2–4]. Up to now, the synthetic methods involving but not limited to hydrothermal, solvothermal, microwave-assisted pyrolysis and electrochemical methods were used to prepare CQDs [5–7]. As we all know, these methods need to be carried out in the liquid phase environment, either involve high-pressure reactions and could cause great hidden dangers, or the products produced have low QY and are not conducive to large-scale production. Therefore, safer, more efficient and suitable for large-scale preparation methods to obtain high-quality CQDs are still required. It is reported that solid-phase pyrolysis is usually an effective route to prepare carbon nanoparticles in a large scale with no need of any reaction solvents [8,9]. For example, Hu et al., through one-pot pyrolysis method in a round bottom flask, prepared carbon dots as eco-friendly nano additives of water-based lubricants to enhance the lubricating property and corrosion inhibition property [10]; Rong et al., through one-pot solid phase pyrolysis methods in a round bottom flask synthesized highly fluorescent CQDs for Fe3+ sensing and cell imaging [11]; Ma et al., prepared blue emitting CQDs by a one-step pyrolysis method, with a high conversion rate (higher than 80%) and high fluorescence QY (up to 88%) [12]. At present, the reaction environment of solid phase pyrolysis method generally includes closed environment and open environment. The reaction vessel used for the former is a hydrothermal reactor, while the vessel used for the latter includes beaker, flask or crucible [13]. The reaction environment has a great influence on the crystallinity, surface defects and emission wavelength of the generated CQDs. Therefore, a comparative study of the CQDs prepared by these two environments is of great guiding significance for the preparation of CQDs by pyrolysis methods in the future. However, as far as we know, there are no relevant reports on this aspect of research. In this work, citric acid and urea, the classical carbon sources, will use as raw materials to prepare two kinds of CQDs respectively in a closed environment (hydrothermal reactor) and an open environment (crucible), and the structure and luminescence mechanism of CQDs will be compared and discussed.

Cephalosporin antibiotics are the most widely used antibiotics in medicine due to their wide antibacterial spectrum and strong bactericidal ability [14,15]. However, because of the abuse of cephalosporin antibiotics and the incomplete treatment of antibiotic wastewater, the content of cephalosporin antibiotics in animal products and the environment is increasing [15–17]. These antibiotics can enrich in the human body through the food chain, and then cause intestinal flora disorders, stomach discomfort, and bacterial resistance and other adverse reactions [18]. Therefore, the monitoring of these antibiotic residues in food and the environmental is very important. Some methods have been reported for the detection of cephalosporin antibiotics such as liquid chromatography [19], micellar electrokinetic chromatography [20], fluorescent polarization immunoassay [21], and spectrophotometry [22]. Among them, liquid chromatography involves expensive equipment and toxic mobile phase reagents [23]; Micellar electrokinetic chromatography requires additional surfactant, which increases the inaccuracy of test results; Fluorescence polarization immunoassay has weak anti-interference and poor stability for complex samples [24]; The accuracy of spectrophotometry is poor [25]. The disadvantages of the method mentioned above are not conducive to its wide application. Thus, the development of a cost-effective, high sensitivity, and portable methods for detection cephalosporin antibiotics is important. Fluorescence nanotechnology has been widely used in various research fields due to its outstanding advantages, and a large number of fluorescent nanoprobe have been developed to sense biomolecules or drug molecules in real-time, and cell imaging, according to the special fluorescence quenching mechanism [26]. In fluorescent nanomaterials, metal-free CQDs have lower cytotoxicity than semiconductor quantum dots and stronger photobleaching resistance than fluorescent dyes, thus showing great application potential in the field of fluorescent probes [27,28]. At present, there is a paucity of CQDs fluorescent probes for the detection of cephalosporin antibiotics.

Herein, citric acid and urea were used as raw materials to prepare two kinds of CQDs with completely different optical properties respectively by pyrolysis in a closed environment (CQDs-1) and an open environment (CQDs-2) for the first time, and the structure and luminescence mechanism of CQDs-1 and CQDs-2 were compared and discussed. The double emission peaks of CQDs-2 excited at 270 nm were used as fluorescence response signals to detect cephalosporin antibiotics based on inner-filter effect (IFE). CQDs-1 and CQDs-2 have been successfully used to image cells due to their high QY, high photostability, and low cytotoxicity. These results indicate that the proposed CQDs will be an outstanding candidate for fluorescent probe and cell imaging.

Citric acid monohydrate (CA) was acquired from Kermel Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Urea was purchased from Tianjin Jindong Tianzheng fine chemical reagent factory. Quinine sulfate dihydrate (AR) was acquired from PAI NI Chemical Reagent Factory (Zhengzhou, China). Sulfuric acid was got from Xi'an Sanpu Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Ceftriaxone sodium (CRO), cefotaxime sodium (CTX), cephalexin (CEX), cephalothin (CF), cefoperazone (CFP) and Cefixime trihydrate (CFM) were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co.,Ltd. Phosphate buffered solution (pH 7.2–7.4) was purchased from Quanzhou Hongke Biotechnology Co., Ltd. HeLa cell line was provided by Shaanxi University of Traditional Chinese Medicine. Thiazolyl blue tetrazolium bromide (MTT) was obtained from Solarbio Life Sciences. Dimethyl sulfoxide was purchased from Tianjin kemeo Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Reagents for interference detection were placed in the Electronic Supplementary Information section. All the reagents were analytical grade and had not undergone further purification. Ultrapure water was used throughout the experiment process.
The instruments information of work was placed in the Electronic Supplementary Information.

The CQDs-1 and CQDs-2 were obtained by one-step pyrolysis in different environments for the first time, as shown in Scheme 1 . In brief, CA (1.05 g, 5 mmol) and urea (1.5 g, 25 mmol) were accurately weighed and placed in a mortar. The mixture was thoroughly ground to obtain the precursor of CQDs. The precursor mixture was placed into a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and kept at 200 °C for 2 h. Cooling down to the room temperature, the collected crude products were dispersed into water (500 mL) and stirred for 10 min until the solid uniformly dispersed. The solution was filtered to remove large particle impurities, and then centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min. Then the supernatant was dialyzed through the dialysis bag (1000 Da, molecular weight cutoff) for 24 h to remove small molecular impurities and obtain blue fluorescence carbon quantum dots (CQDs-1) solution. The solution was dried in an oven at 60 °C to obtain CQDs-1 powder, which was stored at 4 °C for later use.

The preparation method of yellow fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs-2) was the same as the above steps, except that the Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave was replaced with an open crucible, and the reaction time was changed to 40 min.

Key differences between the two methods need to be declared here that: the preparation environment of CQDs-1 is sealed, and at the initial time, the gas in the reaction vessel is air and the pressure is atmospheric, but as the temperature increases and the reaction proceeds, the gas in the vessel will become more complex and may contain water vapor, ammonia and other gases, and the pressure will increase; while the preparation environment of CQDs-2 is an open environment, and the gas during the reaction is always air (even if ammonia or other gases are produced, they will immediately diffuse into the air), and the pressure is always atmospheric. And the preparation time of CQDs-1 and CQDs-2 is different.

The fluorescence QY of CQDs-1 and CQDs-2 were determined by a comparison method [29] with quinine sulfate as standard reference as follows:
(1)
φ x
=
φ s
×
G r a
d x
G r a
d s
×
η x 2
η s 2
(2)
G r a d =
I A

Quinine sulfate was dissolved in 0.1 M sulfuric acid solution, and its QY was 54% [9]. Where
I
,
η
,
φ
and
A
stood for the integral area of fluorescence intensity, refractive index of the solvent, QY, and UV–vis absorbance, respectively.
G r a d
is the slope from the plot of integrated fluorescence intensity against absorbance. The
x
and
s
stood for object and reference, respectively. As shown in Fig. S1, the QY of as-prepared CQDs-1 and CQDs-2 was 49.6% and 32.7%, respectively.

The cephalosporin antibiotics used in this work included CRO, CTX, CEX, CF, CFP and CFM. Their structure is shown in Fig. 1 . Taking CRO as an example, the typical detection procedure is as follows: The CQDs-2 was dispersed in PBS buffer solution (pH = 7.2–7.4) to prepare a 10 μg/mL solution of CQDs-2. Different concentrations of CRO stock solutions (0.5 mL) were added to 4.5 mL as-prepared CQDs-2 solution, and the final concentration of CRO was 0–45 μM, followed by incubation at room temperature for 1 min, and then the fluorescence spectra of these solutions were measured and recorded. The final concentration of CTX, CEX, CF, CFP and CFM were 0–100 μM, 0–150 μM, 0–65 μM, 0–150 μM and 0–90 μM, respectively. To ensure the credibility of the data, all tests were performed in three parallel groups.

In short, commercial milk (10 mL) was diluted with acetonitrile (20 mL) and the mixture was stirred for 15 min and then centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 3 min [30]. The collected supernatant was then filtered through a microporous membrane with a diameter of 0.22 μm. Finally, the filtrate was diluted 10 times with PBS buffer solution (pH = 7.2–7.4). The measurement method is the same as section 2.5. Measurements were conducted in triplicate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell imaging of CQDs-1 and CQDs-2
ce:section.ce:para:
The cell imaging study of CQDs was carried out on Hela cell, and the cytotoxicity of carbon dots was first evaluated by MTT experiment. The details of the MTT experiment are in the Electronic Supplementary Information. The cell viability was calculated according to the following equation:
(3)
Cell viability
( % )
=
Absorbance treated
Absorbance control
× 100 %
Three parallel tests were conducted out in three generations of cells to prove the credibility of the experiment.

The next experiment was performed cell imaging study of CQDs to Hela cell. When HeLa cells grew to 80% in the culture flask, the medium was removed and the cells were washed with PBS buffer solution for 3 times. Then 2 mL cells were added to each well of the 6-well plate with a density of 1 × 106 cells/mL. The cells were cultured for 16–18 h. The supernatant was removed and the cells were washed with PBS buffer for 3 times. Then 1.8 mL 1640 medium was added to each well and 0.2 mL CQDs (10 μg/mL) were added. After 24 h of culture, the supernatant was discarded. The cells were washed with PBS buffer for 3 times, and fluorescence image was collected on a confocal laser-scanning microscope.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The influence of pyrolysis environment on the prepared CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Blue fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs-1) and yellow fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs-2) were prepared by pyrolyzing the mixture of CA and urea in closed environment and open environment, respectively. The synthesis process was shown in Scheme 1. By adjusting the reaction conditions, it was finally determined that the optimal reaction conditions of the CQDs-2 including the molar ratio of CA to urea, reaction temperature, and reaction time were 1:5, 200 °C, and 40 min, respectively (Fig. S2). The 3D fluorescence spectra of CQDS-2 at different reaction temperatures and times were further monitored. As shown in Figs. S3a–f, with the reaction temperature increased from 120 to 220 °C, the 3D fluorescence of CQDs-2 gradually red-shifted. The CQDs-2 solution gradually changed from blue to yellow (Fig. S3g) under 365 nm irradiation, and its emission center red-shifted from 444 nm to 513 nm (Figs. S3h and i). The effect of reaction time on the fluorescence of CQDs-2 was similar to that of reaction temperature. As shown in Figs. S4a–f, with the reaction time extended from 5 min to 50 min, the 3D fluorescence of CQDs-2 gradually red-shifted. The CQDs-2 solution gradually changed from blue to yellow (Fig. S4g) under 365 nm irradiation, and its emission center red-shifted from 444 nm to 511 nm (Figs. S4h and i). This was related to the higher degree of carbonization as the reaction time increases. It was confirmed in the subsequent Fig. 4a and Fig. 4b. For CQDs-1, it was finally determined that the optimal reaction conditions of the CQDs-1 including the molar ratio of CA to urea, reaction temperature, and reaction time were 1:5, 200 °C, and 2 h, respectively (Fig. S5). It can be seen that under the optimization reaction conditions, the fluorescence of CQDs-1 only changes in emission intensity, while the emission position is almost unchanged. This proves that the reaction environment has a great influence on the optical properties of CQDs.

To investigate the fluorescence behavior of CQDs more intuitively, three-dimensional fluorescent spectroscopy was performed. As shown in Fig. 2 a and Fig. 2b, CQDs-1 has an obvious luminescent center at (405 nm, 460 nm), while CQDs-2 has four obvious luminescent centers at (270 nm, 370 nm), (270 nm, 520 nm), (325 nm, 370 nm) and (405 nm, 520 nm), respectively. The UV–vis absorption spectra (Fig. 2c) and two-dimensional fluorescence spectra (Fig. 2d) match well with the results of three-dimensional fluorescence spectroscopy. The excitation center of CQDs-1 is at 405 nm, and the emission center is at 460 nm. The excitation center of CQDs-2 was located at 270 nm, 325 nm and 405 nm, respectively. The optimal emission center was located at 520 nm, but when the excitation center was 270 nm, CQDs-2 showed double emission at 370 nm and 520 nm, respectively. Considering the above results, the excitation regions at 270 nm, 325 nm and 405 nm may be attributed to the intrinsic “carbon core” (π-π* transition), “edge state” (n-π* transition) and “surface state” (surface defect or fluorophore), respectively [31–33]. Compared to CQDs-1, CQDs-2 has the intrinsic emission of a carbon core. In general, the surface states give CQDs the characteristics of excitation dependence [34]. As shown in Fig. S6, emission wavelengths of CQDs at different excitation wavelengths are tested. It can be seen that the emission peaks of CQDs-1 and CQDs-2 moved with the excitation wavelength. It proved that both CQDs-1 and CQDs-2 had a certain proportion of surface state emission. Combined with the above results, it can be inferred that CQDs-1 has more surface state emission than CQDs-2. In order to confirm the conjecture and further understand the reasons for the difference in fluorescence of CQDs prepared in the two environments, they were characterized by TEM, XRD, Raman, FT-IR, XPS and fluorescence lifetime decay.

TEM experiments were conducted to investigate the morphology and sizes of CQDs-1 and CQDs-2. As shown in Fig. 3 a and Fig. 3c, both the CQDs-1 and CQDs-2 were well dispersed and regular in size. Inserts of Fig. 3a and c were the distribution histograms, which illustrated that the size of the CQDs-1 and CQDs-2 was about 1.28–3.10 nm and 1.27–2.73 nm, with an average size of 2.08 nm and 2.09 nm, respectively. The High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image of CQDs-1 (Fig. 3b) and CQDs-2 (Fig. 3d) revealed the interlayer lattice spacing was 0.23 nm, which represented the diffraction planes of graphitic carbon [11,35]. The crystal structures of CQDs-1 and CQDs-2 were studied by XRD and Raman. As illustrated in Fig. 4a, the XRD spectrum of CQDs-1 and CQDs-2 showed a broad peak centered at around 27°, which is attributed to the (002) planes of graphite. The XRD half-peak width of CQDs-2 is narrower than that of CQDs-1, so it proved that CQDs-2 has higher crystallinity than CQDs-1. The Raman spectrum (excited at 532 nm) (Fig. 4b) of CQDs-1 and CQDs-2 displays a disordered D-band at 1346 cm−1 and an ordered G-band at 1567 cm−1. In general, the value of IG/ID is used to evaluate the degree of graphitization of the CQDs, and larger IG/ID indicates a higher degree of graphitization of the CQDs. The IG/ID ratio of CQDs-1 and CQDs-2 were 0.88 and 1.12, respectively, indicating the higher degree of graphitization of CQDs-2 than that of CQDs-1 [36,37], which is consistent with the results of XRD. The FTIR spectrum of CQDs-1 and CQDs-2 was presented in Fig. 4c. The stretching vibrations for –OH and –NH2 groups (3300-3000 cm−1), C–H (2978 cm−1 and 2900 cm−1), C=O (1652 cm−1), C=C (1558 cm−1), C–O–C (1340 cm−1), C–N (1188 cm−1) and C–O (1049 cm−1) reveal the existence of conjugated aromatic structure composed of N- and O- containing groups [38–41]. And it can also be seen that the content of these functional groups is slightly different. Therefore, the composition of CQDs-1 and CQDs-2 was further quantitatively analyzed by XPS. As shown in Fig. 5 a, the XPS survey spectra of the CQDs samples demonstrate three main peaks, which correspond to the C 1s (285.06 eV), N 1s (400.01 eV), and O 1s (531.85 eV). The proportion of carbon element in CQDs-2 is higher than that in CQDs-1 (for the proportion data see Table S1). The high resolution XPS spectra (Fig. 5b) of C 1s confirmed the existence of the C=C/C–C bond (284.81 eV), C–N/C–O bond (285.54 eV), and C=N/C=O bond (288.26 eV) [42]. The N 1s (Fig. 5c) high resolution spectrum can be deconvoluted into C=N (399.93 eV) and N–H (401.76 eV) [43]. The O 1s (Fig. 5d) was fitted into C=O (531.64 eV) and C–O (533.21 eV) [44,45]. The high-resolution XPS proportion data was shown in Table S2. It can be found that the ratio of C=O and C=N of CQDs-2 is higher than that of CQDs-1. This also indicated that the CQDs formed by pyrolysis in an open environment had a higher conjugate structure than those formed by pyrolysis in a closed environment. Therefore, it is believed that the difference in the conjugate structure in the both CQDs is another reason for their luminescence difference. This is because the large conjugate structure will lead to a smaller band gap, which will lead to the red shift of fluorescence emission [46,47]. The size of the conjugate domain will affect the crystallinity of the carbon dots. Generally speaking, the carbon quantum dots with higher conjugate domain will also have higher crystallinity. The above results including TEM, XRD, Raman, and XPS confirmed that the crystallinity and graphitization degree of CQDs-2 was higher than that of CQDs-1. This may be due to the fact that CQDs precursor will generate more CO2 gas and NO2 gas with air in the high temperature and open environment, and consume more oxygen and nitrogen elements, resulting in higher crystallinity and graphitization degree of the CQDs. It is also observed from Table 1 that the content of N and O in CQDs-2 is lower than that in CQDs-1. It may be that more oxygen and nitrogen are consumed in the form of oxide gases. Generally speaking, the proportion of surface state emission will be relatively reduced with the increasing of crystallinity. Therefore, the fluorescence lifetime decay is carried out to prove the above speculation. As shown in Fig. 4d and Table S5, the CQDs-1 and CQDs-2 showed a biexponential process with fast (τ1) and slow (τ2) decay components to the recombination from the core state (π-π*) and the surface states (n-π*), respectively [48,49]. The results showed that CQDs-1 had more surface state emission than CQDs-2, while CQDs-2 had higher core state emission than CQDs-1, which also confirmed the previous prediction. The QY of CQDs-1 and CQDs-2 was determined by the five-point method with quinine sulfate as a reference. As shown in Fig. S1, the QY of CQDs-1 and CQDs-2 were 49.6% and 32.7%, respectively. The mass yields of CQDs-1 and CQDs-2 were 14.67% and 26.06%, respectively, using the total mass of CA (1.05 g) and urea (1.5 g) as the mass of the raw material. In short, the CQDs formed by pyrolysis in an open environment had a higher crystallinity, graphitization degree and less surface state than those formed by pyrolysis in a closed environment, which may be the reasons for different luminescence properties of the both CQDs.

The photochemical stability and colloidal stability of fluorescent probe are crucial for the application of fluorescent nanomaterials in the actual environment. Therefore, the pH resistance, photobleaching resistance, and salt resistance were carried out. As shown in Fig. S7a, CQDs-1 and CQDs-2 had similar performance in pH 1–14 solution. The acidic environment had little effect on the fluorescence intensity of the CQDs, while the alkaline environment had a great quenching effect on the fluorescence intensity. Since pH can affect the surface states of CQDs, it also indicated that both kinds of CQDs contain partial surface state emission. Figs. S7b and c showed that the fluorescence intensity of the CQDs solution not only under UV irradiation for up to 5 h, but also in the NaCl solution with a concentration as high as 1 mol/L did not change significantly. Therefore, CQDs-1 and CQDs-2 had the potential for uninterrupted tracking studies.

As a matter of fact, CQDs-2 has a double emission characteristic under 270 nm excitation, so it has a greater advantage in terms of fluorescence probe compared with CQDs-1. Anions, cations, biomolecules and antibiotics that may be present in the organism and in food were used as interferents (Including but not limited to Cl−, NO3 −, SO4 2−, K+, Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Al3+, L-Cys, Gly, Glucose, L-Phe, L-Ser, L-Ala, L-Asp, L-Lys, AA, L-Arg, FA, UA, Urea, GSH, H2O2, TC, AMOX, CRO, CTX, CEX, CF, CFP, CFM, PG) to investigate their effects on the fluorescence intensity of CQDs-2. As shown in Fig. S8, CQDs-2 had no apparent response to numerous molecules and ions (1 mmol/L) except cephalosporin antibiotics.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The dual emission fluorescent probe detection cephalosporin antibiotics
ce:section.ce:para:
Fluorescence sensing experiments based on CQDs-2 detection cephalosporin antibiotics including CRO, CTX, CEX, CF, CFP, and CFM were performed. The initial pH value of CQDs-2 solution was 7, and CQDs-2 had the best fluorescence intensity in neutral solution. Therefore, the detection environment was set as neutral environment, which is also consistent with the pH value of most actual samples. The best detection time for detection samples was investigated. As shown in Figs. S9 and 1 min after the adding cephalosporin antibiotics, the fluorescence intensity of CQDs-2 no longer changes significantly. Therefore, the fluorescence data of the system was measured when the cephalosporin antibiotics was added to CQDs-2 solution for 1 min. Taking ceftriaxone sodium as an example, fluorescent quenching of CQDs measurements were performed after 1 min of reaction with different concentrations of CRO (0–45 μM). As shown in Fig. 6 a, it can be seen that the F370 and F520 of CQDs-2 were gradually decreased with the concentrations of CRO increasing. The fluorescence quenching efficiency Diff/Diff0 (Diff = F520–F370) was highly proportional to the range of CRO concentration (0–45 μM, R2 = 0.988) with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.89 μM. The LOD of CRO was calculated according to the following Eq. (4) [50]:
(4)
L O D =
3 σ
k

Where σ is the standard deviation for ten repeated fluorescence measurements of the CQDs solution in the absence of CRO, and k denotes the slope of standard curve. Several other cephalosporin antibiotics showed similar quenching behavior to the fluorescence CQDs-2. Similarly, the detection ranges of CTX (Fig. 6b), CEX (Fig. 6c), CF (Fig. 6d), CFP (Fig. 6e) and CFM (Fig. 6f) were 0–100 μM, 0–150 μM, 0–65 μM, 0–150 μM and 0–90 μM, respectively, with detection limits of 0.93 μM, 2.78 μM, 1.19 μM, 1.67 μM and 1.67 μM, respectively. Further, the anti-interference ability of the sensor was evaluated. As shown in Fig. S10, taking CRO as an example, the coexistence of interferers and CRO does not significantly affect the fluorescence quenching degree of CQDS-2 by CRO. The results proved that CQDs-2 had excellent anti-interference performance in the detection of cephalosporin antibiotics. As shown in Table 1, compared with the previously reported methods for the detection of cephalosporins antibiotics [18,51–56], the fluorescent probe provided in this work can detect more cephalosporin antibiotics with a wider detection range and a lower detection limit. And because more cephalosporin antibiotics can be detected, the fluorescent probe has more practical application value for the detection of cephalosporin antibiotics.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of CQDs-2 detection of cephalosporins antibiotics
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the sensing mechanism for the CQDs-2 dual emission fluorescent probe detection of cephalosporins antibiotics, taking CRO as an example, the three-dimensional fluorescence spectra of CQDs-2 before and after adding CRO were determined. As can be seen from Fig. 7 a and b, the emission region before the excitation wavelength of 325 nm disappeared after the addition of CRO, while the emission region at the excitation wavelength of 405 nm almost remained unchanged. Therefore, it can be seen that CRO only quenched the fluorescence with the excitation wavelength less than 325 nm. The two-dimensional fluorescence spectra of CQDs-2 and CQDs-2+CRO excited at 270 nm, 325 nm, 365 nm and 405 nm were further tested. As shown in Fig. S11, the fluorescence excited at 270 nm was completely quenched (Fig. S11a), while the fluorescence excited at 325 nm was partially quenched (Fig. S11b), and the fluorescence excited at 365 nm and 405 nm was not quenched (Figs. S11c and d). Furthermore, the UV absorption of cephalosporins was tested. As shown in Fig. 7c, cephalosporin antibiotics have strong absorption before 325 nm, while the excitation center of the double emission peak of CQDs-2 was located at 270 nm. The UV absorption of the quencher coincides with the excitation center of the fluorescein, which is a characteristic of the inner-filter effect [57]. Therefore, the fluorescence quenching may be attributed to the inner-filter effect. On the one hand, fluorescence quenching based on the inner-filter effect will not generate new substances [58], so the ultraviolet absorption spectrum of the mixture of CQDs and cephalosporin antibiotics was tested. As shown in Fig. S12, it can be seen that only the synergistic effect of CQDs and cephalosporin leads to an increase in absorption intensity, and no new peak appears. On the other hand, fluorescence quenching based on inner-filter effect does not cause significant changes in fluorescence lifetime [59]. Therefore, the fluorescence lifetime decay of the mixture of CQDs-2 and CRO was tested. As shown in Fig. 7d, the fluorescence lifetime of CQDs-2+CRO was almost the same as that of CQDs-2 (the data was listed in Table S6.). Therefore, based on the above results, the fluorescence quenching mechanism can be determined as inner-filter effect. Because the excitation center is located at 405 nm, CQDS-1 cannot form an inner-filter effect with cephalosporin antibiotics to resulting in fluorescence quenching, as shown in Fig. S13. Therefore, CQDs-1 is not suitable for detecting cephalosporin antibiotics.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of CRO in real sample
ce:section.ce:para: To test its practicability, the CQDs-2 fluorescent probe was applied for monitoring CRO in milk samples. After pretreatments as described in the experimental section, milk sample are spiked with standard solutions of CRO (10, 20, 30 μM). As listed in Table 2 , the recoveries of CRO were calculated to be 99.4%–102%, with RSD of 2.03–9.64%. Therefore, above results demonstrate feasibility of CQDs-2 fluorescent probe.

Cytotoxicity is an important evaluation index to evaluate the feasibility of biological application of CQDs. Therefore, the cytotoxicity of CQDs-1 and CQDs-2 to Hela cells was evaluated by MTT experiment. As shown in Fig. S14, the cell activity still exceeded 85% in the presence of concentrations as high as 40 μg/mL of CQDs for 24 h. The data confirmed that the CQDs-1 and CQDs-2 exhibited a low cytotoxicity and excellent biocompatibility and could be applied in cell imaging. Subsequently, the cell imaging pictures were captured by a laser confocal microscope. As shown in Figs. S15a–d, it is clear observed that the cells cultured with the CQDs-1 showed significant blue, yellow and red fluorescence under different excitation channel, respectively. However, the cells cultured with CQDs-2 showed extraordinary weak blue fluorescence and significant yellow and red fluorescence (Figs. S15e–h). This is due to that the best emission wavelength of CQDs-2 is located at 520 nm, which belongs to the range of green and yellow fluorescence, while the best emission wavelength of CQDs-1 is located at 460 nm, which belongs to the range of blue fluorescence. The images indicated that CQDs-1 and CQDs −2 can penetrated into the cells and could maintain a steady and clear fluorescent signal. These results illustrated that the CQDs-1 and CQDs-2 nanoprobe could be expected as an excellent potential agent for bioimaging applications.

In summary, by controlling reaction environment, blue fluorescence carbon quantum dots (CQDs-1) and yellow fluorescence carbon quantum dots (CQDs-2) were successfully synthesized by one-pot solid phase pyrolysis method in autoclaves and crucibles, respectively. The CQDs prepared in open environment have longer emission wavelength, higher crystallinity and less surface states emission than those prepared in closed environment. CQDs-1 and CQDs-2 had almost the same particle size, 2.08 nm and 2.09 nm, respectively. The obtained QY of the CQDs-1 was 49.55% and that of CQDs-2 was 32.73%. Based on the inner-filter effect, CQDs-2 was used to detect cephalosporin antibiotics including CRO, CTX, CEX, CF, CFP and CFM, with detection limits of 0.87 μM, 0.93 μM, 2.78 μM, 1.19 μM, 1.67 μM and 1.67 μM, respectively. Furthermore, CQDs-2 were explored to quantify the concentration of CRO in real milk samples with good rate of recovery (99.4%–102%). Moreover, the CQDs-1 and CQDs-2 were shown to be biocompatible and useful as a cell imaging. In a word, this work not only explained the mechanism of the influence of different reaction environment on the preparation of CQDs, but also provides a better idea for the preparation of CQDs by pyrolysis in the future. In addition, the multifunctional fluorescent sensor based on CQDs prepared by pyrolysis methods have good prospects for sensing and bio-imaging.

Cunjin Wang: Roles, Writing – original draft. Min Yang: Conceptualization. Huanxian Shi: Investigation. Zhuoru Yao: Investigation. Enzhou Liu: Investigation. Xiaoyun Hu: Funding acquisition. Pengqi Guo: Methodology. Weiming Xue: Supervision. Jun Fan: Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 22078261 and 11974276), the Project funded by China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (No. 2020M683701XB), and the Natural Science Foundation of Shaanxi Province (No. 2020JQ-609, 2018JM2037). The authors would also like to thank Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for the fluorescence lifetime and Raman analysis.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Kulapina OI, Makarova NM, Kulapina EG. Potentiometric sensors for the determination of some cephalosporin antibiotics in biological fluids and medicinal preparations. Journal of Analytical Chemistry. 2015;70(4):477-484.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Application of aluminum hydroxide for improvement of label-free SERS detection of some cephalosporin antibiotics in urine
Biosensors

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Markina NE, Markin AV. Application of Aluminum Hydroxide for Improvement of Label-Free SERS Detection of Some Cephalosporin Antibiotics in Urine. Biosensors 2019;9(3):91.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ratiometric determination of Cr(VI) based on a dual-emission fluorescent nanoprobe using carbon quantum dots and a smartphone app
Mikrochim Acta

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Yao C, Liu Q, Zhao N, Liu JM, Fang G, Wang S. Ratiometric determination of Cr(VI) based on a dual-emission fluorescent nanoprobe using carbon quantum dots and a smartphone app. Mikrochim Acta. 2021;188(3):89.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Inner filter effect-based sensor for horseradish peroxidase and its application to fluorescence immunoassay
ACS Sens

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Sun J, Zhao J, Wang L, Li H, Yang F, Yang X. Inner Filter Effect-Based Sensor for Horseradish Peroxidase and Its Application to Fluorescence Immunoassay. ACS Sens. 2018;3(1):183-190.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Black phosphorus quantum dots as the ratiometric fluorescence probe for trace mercury ion detection based on inner filter effect
ACS Sens

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Gu W, Pei X, Cheng Y, Zhang C, Zhang J, Yan Y, et al. Black Phosphorus Quantum Dots as the Ratiometric Fluorescence Probe for Trace Mercury Ion Detection Based on Inner Filter Effect. ACS Sensors. 2017;2(4):576-582.
